title: Cat Yellow Nasal Discharge: Causes, Urgency, and Next Steps
meta_desc: Is your cat's nose running with yellow discharge? Learn about causes, why it is a medium-level urgency, and how photos help with veterinary triage.
content_html:
TL;DR: Yellow nasal discharge in cats typically indicates a bacterial infection or inflammation requiring a veterinary exam within 24-48 hours, though difficulty breathing or extreme lethargy necessitates immediate urgent care.

What does yellow nasal discharge mean for my cat?

Yellow nasal discharge in cats, often referred to as mucopurulent discharge, typically indicates an active inflammatory process or a secondary bacterial infection. While clear discharge might be related to mild allergies, the shift to a thick yellow or greenish color suggests that white blood cells are responding to an underlying issue such as a feline upper respiratory infection (URI), feline herpesvirus, or even dental disease that has affected the nasal passages.

Is my cat having an emergency if their nasal discharge is yellow?

The urgency level for yellow nasal discharge is considered Medium. Use the following guidelines to determine the best course of action:

  • Schedule a veterinary appointment within 24 to 48 hours if your cat is still breathing easily, eating, and behaving normally.
  • Seek urgent care immediately if the discharge is accompanied by a complete loss of appetite, extreme lethargy, or open-mouth breathing.

Why should I provide a photo of my cat's nasal discharge for triage?

Providing a clear, close-up photo of your cat’s nose and the discharge can be a vital tool for a triage professional or veterinarian. A photo allows the medical team to:

  • Assess the thickness and volume of the fluid.
  • Determine if the discharge is unilateral (one nostril) or bilateral (both nostrils).
  • Identify diagnostic clues, such as distinguishing between systemic infections (bilateral) and localized issues like foreign objects, polyps, or dental abscesses (unilateral).

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cats, yellow nasal discharge (mucopurulent) can be caused by various conditions, including acute unilateral discharge suggestive of a foreign body, or chronic nasal discharge potentially indicating neoplastic or mycotic disease. Feline viral rhinotracheitis can also cause mucopurulent nasal discharge, often accompanied by fever, sneezing, conjunctivitis, salivation, depression, and anorexia. Diagnosis is based on history, physical examination, radiographic findings (especially CT), rhinoscopy, nasal biopsy, deep nasal tissue culture, and elimination of other causes. Advanced imaging and biopsy may identify a specific diagnosis, such as fungal rhinitis, neoplasia, or foreign body. Serum titers for cryptococcal antigen are useful for diagnosing nasal cryptococcosis.

Chapter: Respiratory, Infectious Diseases, Neoplasia, Fungal

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1489)

faqs.question: Can yellow nasal discharge go away on its own?
faqs.answer: While some viral infections may eventually resolve, yellow discharge usually indicates a secondary bacterial infection that requires veterinary-prescribed antibiotics to clear properly.

faqs.question: What if my cat is also sneezing or has runny eyes?
faqs.answer: Sneezing and ocular discharge are common signs of feline respiratory complexes. These symptoms should be reported to your vet as they help determine the specific type of treatment needed.

faqs.question: Is yellow nasal discharge contagious to other cats?
faqs.answer: Yes, many causes of yellow nasal discharge are infectious. It is best to isolate the affected cat from other household pets until they have been evaluated by a veterinarian.
